retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Vasopressors
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressors may be substituted when a patient requires more doses of epinephrine but have unacceptable side effects from the epinephrine intravenous infusion (arrhythmia or chest pain). If no clear second-line pressor is identified, treatment guidelines would follow those of any other patient in hypotensive shock.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Triage
retrieved_docs.content: Triage any allergic reaction with urgency as they are at risk for rapid deterioration with the development of anaphylaxis, if not already anaphylactic. [15] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Combining an H1 antihistamine with an H2 antihistamine, such as famotidine, may help patients achieve a better reduction in urticaria symptoms. However, antihistamines should not be used as monotherapy, as they do not mitigate upper or lower airway obstruction, shock, or hypotension. Antihistamines have not been shown to affect hospitalization rates or epinephrine requirements. Some studies suggest that when H2 antagonists are given IV, they may increase rates of hypotension. The recommended dose of famotidine is 20 mg for adults and 0.25 mg/kg for children, up to a maximum dose of 20 mg.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis is a life-threatening emergency that demands immediate action. If possible, remove the triggering agent right away. Administering intramuscular (IM) epinephrine into the lateral thigh without delay is the most critical step. Repeat IM or intravenous (IV) doses may be needed. Unless prevented by airway swelling, the patient should be placed in a supine position with legs elevated to support blood flow. For pregnant patients, the left lateral position is used to avoid compressing the vena cava. These positions help maintain organ perfusion and prevent sudden cardiovascular collapse.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Other Vasopressors
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians may consider additional vasopressors such as norepinephrine, vasopressin, or dopamine for patients with refractory hypotension.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: High-flow oxygen should be administered using a nonrebreather mask at 15 L/min, and IV access should be established promptly. For stable patients, IV fluids should be started at 125 mL/h of normal saline for adults and at weight-based maintenance rates for children. Intubation should be anticipated in the presence of any respiratory distress, stridor, voice changes, or visible airway swelling. For patients who remain hypotensive after epinephrine, clinicians should begin aggressive fluid resuscitation. Adults typically require 1 to 2 L of normal saline rapidly, whereas children require 20 mL/kg boluses over 5 to 10 min, repeated as needed. Lactated Ringer may lead to metabolic alkalosis; however, large volumes of normal saline can cause hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, so some experts choose to switch to lactated Ringer when large volumes of normal saline are necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- IV Fluid Resuscitation
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis induces a distributive shock that typically is responsive to fluid resuscitation and the above epinephrine. One to 2 L or 10 to 20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus should be given for observed hypotension. Albumin or hypertonic solutions are not indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Adjunctive Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: When anaphylaxis is diagnosed, co-treatment is often initiated with steroids, antihistamines, inhaled bronchodilators, and vasopressors. Glucagon can also be used if indicated. These agents can assist in refractory initial anaphylaxis or prevent recurrence and biphasic reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- An Evidence-based Approach to Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis is a rapid systemic and unpredictable disorder that is life-threatening. The disorder often occurs after exposure to certain antigens, mostly insect venom, food, or medications. The condition is immune-mediated and rapidly induces symptoms. For those who have not encountered anaphylaxis, the diagnosis can be difficult. In the United States, the rates of anaphylaxis have doubled over the past 20 years, and at least 1500 people die from the disorder each year. The potentially life-threatening nature of the disorder makes it imperative that one be prepared and offer prompt treatment. This is 1 of the few medical disorders during which a healthcare worker does not have the luxury of time. Having epinephrine on hand can be life-saving if one wants to avoid a fatal outcome. However, despite considerable evidence that epinephrine is the drug of choice, it is under-used. Instead, many healthcare workers use an antihistamine, a drug that does not prevent or relieve all the manifestations of anaphylaxis. [20] In addition, the antihistamine does not even start to work for 1 to 3 hours, depending on the route of administration. Given these findings, all healthcare workers must be educated on anaphylaxis and its treatment. Evidence indicates that healthcare workers are critical for managing and preventing anaphylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Shock -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Septic shock - initial aggressive fluid resuscitation with IV isotonic crystalloids 30 mL/kg within 3 hrs with additional fluid based on frequent reassessment, empiric antibiotic therapy within one hr. [8] For patients with septic shock requiring vasopressors, target a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 65 mmHg. The first choice of a vasopressor is norepinephrine, with the addition of vasopressin if refractory. [9] Anaphylactic shock - aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with 4 to 6 L of IV crystalloids. Stop the offending agent, intramuscular epinephrine, antihistamines, corticosteroids, nebulized albuterol. In adrenal crisis - judicious fluid resuscitation, IV dexamethasone.

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis treatment begins with triage and the removal from exposure, which can involve decontamination if topical agents are involved or clothing is contaminated. Removal of the patient's clothing and standard precautions by staff such as masks, gloves, and gown should take place. Hand washing by staff who may have used latex gloves or eaten peanuts may be necessary. ABC’s (airway, breathing, and circulation) of emergency care should have priority.  Patients should have oxygen and intravenous administration of fluids. The patient should receive epinephrine intramuscularly (IM) (1 to 1000 dilution {1 mg/1ml} 0.3cc) 0.3 to 0.5 mg which can be repeated every 5 to 15 minutes as needed. Most patients should respond to 1 to 3 doses if symptoms persist, progress or the patient becomes hypotensive IV epinephrine should be started at 0.1 mcg/kg/minute by infusion device and titrated to maintain perfusion of body organs. Patients on beta-blockers should be given glucagon 1 to 5 mg over 5 minutes. Antiemetics should be given to treat vomiting often associated with glucagon administration. Histamine type 2 receptor blockers such as famotidine 20 mg IV or ranitidine at  50 mg IV should be administered. The glucocorticoid, methylprednisolone 125 mg, should be given. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolone (80 to 125 mg IV) or hydrocortisone (250 to 500 mg IV) are the accepted treatments during the acute phase, after which oral treatment of prednisone (40 to 60 mg daily or divided twice per day) is continued for 3 to 5 days. Again, if the source is unknown or there is a concern for a prolonged time before physician follow-up, a steroid taper of up to 2 weeks may be provided. Mineralocorticoid activity is responsible for fluid retention; dexamethasone and methylprednisolone are the preferred agents for those at risk as they induce the least mineralocorticoid effect.

retrieved_docs.title: Adverse Drug Reactions -- Clinical Significance -- Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis constitutes a medical emergency. The offending agent should be immediately stopped, and IM epinephrine should be administered promptly. [66] Simultaneous oxygen administration, fluid resuscitation, and albuterol nebulization should be provided. Adjunctive therapies include intravenous antihistamines (histamine 1 and 2 receptor antagonists) and intravenous corticosteroids. If there is evidence of impending airway obstruction or compromise, intubation is required. In the case of anaphylactic shock, the patient may require additional fluid and vasopressor administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective management of type I hypersensitivity requires coordinated, patient-centered care supported by strong interprofessional collaboration. Clinicians and advanced practitioners play a key role in diagnosis, risk stratification, and treatment planning. Their responsibilities include obtaining a detailed allergy history, ordering appropriate diagnostic tests, and initiating evidence-based interventions such as epinephrine for anaphylaxis or allergen immunotherapy when indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Glucagon
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians may use glucagon for refractory anaphylaxis, particularly in patients who are taking β-blockers, as these medications can reduce the effectiveness of epinephrine. Glucagon bypasses β-adrenergic receptors by directly activating adenylate cyclase, thereby increasing intracellular cyclic AMP, which improves cardiac output and reduces bronchospasm. Common adverse effects include nausea and vomiting; therefore, placing the patient in the left lateral decubitus position is advisable to prevent aspiration. The dosing of glucagon is as follows: Adults receive 1 to 5 mg IV over 5 minutes. Pediatric patients receive 20 to 30 µg/kg (maximum 1 mg) IV over 5 minutes. Following the initial dose, both adults and children can receive an infusion of 5 to 15 µg/min, titrated to achieve the desired effect. [26] [44] [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Immediate Hypersensitivity Reactions (Archived) -- Complications -- Anaphylactic Shock
retrieved_docs.content: This is an allergic reaction, often life-threatening, triggered by an allergen to which the immune system overreacts. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2166
retrieved_docs.content: Butenafine, an antifungal medication, is contraindicated in patients who have فرط تحسس (hypersensitivity), meaning it should not be prescribed to individuals with a known allergic or hypersensitivity reaction to avoid adverse immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: swelling and bronchoconstriction, highlighting its potential therapeutic role in histamine‑mediated conditions. This taxonomy clarifies systemic versus inhaled routes, adrenergic subtypes, and the composition of combination bronchodilator products, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for optimal management of obstructive airway diseases and related allergic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 340
retrieved_docs.content: emphasizing the need for clinicians to recognize and prevent hypersensitivity‑related adverse events across the entire spectrum of listed pharmaceuticals. Overall, the integrated framework unifies diverse drug classes through a structural‑analogue taxonomy, connects them to a diagnostic radiopharmaceutical, and provides explicit hypersensitivity contraindications, thereby supporting consistent clinical documentation, safe prescribing and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1849
retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa is classified as a centrally acting antiadrenergic agent and is a member of the methyldopa drug class. Use of methyldopa is contraindicated in patients who have a hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس) reaction to the medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 87
retrieved_docs.content: strategy but also mandates clinician awareness of possible hypersensitivity or adverse reactions when selecting or switching among these medications. Clinically, recognizing the full spectrum of tetracycline‑class agents, their dosage forms, strengths, and shared excipients supports appropriate drug selection, dosing, and monitoring for allergic or cross‑reactive events, thereby optimizing patient safety and therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate is identified as a potential therapeutic agent for the management of shock. Shock, a life‑threatening circulatory failure, is classified in medical terminology as “صدمة إنتانيّة” (infectious shock) and also as “صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ” (cardiogenic shock), reflecting its distinct etiologic subtypes. Recognizing that cortisol succinate may be used to treat these severe shock states underscores its relevance in acute care settings where rapid reversal of hemodynamic collapse is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: antagonist distinct from the other H1‑blocking agents. Overall, the taxonomy illustrates how individual drugs—ranging from antiallergic nasal agents, plain and decongestant sympathomimetics, adrenergic substances, H1‑histamine antagonists, to specialized therapies for hereditary angioedema and rare musculoskeletal disorders—are organized into overlapping classes that guide precise therapeutic selection and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 687
retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa is identified as a centrally acting antiadrenergic agent and is a member of the class of centrally acting antiadrenergic agents, which in turn are a subclass of antiadrenergic agents that act on the central nervous system. Both Methyldopa and the antifungal agents butenafine and tolnaftate are contraindicated in patients who have hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). This underscores the clinical need to assess for allergic reactions before prescribing Methyldopa, butenafine, or tolnaftate, as each of these drugs should be avoided in individuals with a known hypersensitivity to the respective medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2051
retrieved_docs.content: Quercetin may be used to treat الحساسية الغذائية (food allergy), as it possesses anti‑inflammatory and antihistamine properties that can help alleviate allergic reactions triggered by food allergens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Atrakurium, imatinib mesylát, and etodolac are each contraindicated in the presence of فرط الحساسية الدوائية (drug hypersensitivity). This shared contraindication underscores the necessity of evaluating a patient’s allergy history before prescribing any of these medications, as exposure in individuals with drug hypersensitivity could precipitate severe allergic reactions. Proper assessment and avoidance of these agents in susceptible patients are essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1695
retrieved_docs.content: Butenafine and tolnaftát are both antifungal agents that should be avoided in patients who have a hypersensitivity condition (فرط تحسس). The presence of hypersensitivity is a contraindication for the use of each drug, emphasizing the need to evaluate a patient’s allergy history before prescribing either medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 306
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone acetate, a corticosteroid formulation, may be employed in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). By providing anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, dexamethasone acetate can help modulate the excessive systemic inflammatory response that characterizes septic shock, potentially improving hemodynamic stability and patient outcomes when used according to appropriate clinical guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: Antipruritika are agents that alleviate itching, and ketotifen is classified as an antipruritic medication. As a member of the antipruritika group, ketotifen is used to reduce pruritus associated with allergic and dermatologic conditions, often acting as a mast‑cell stabilizer and antihistamine to control histamine‑mediated itch.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Tolnaftate is contraindicated in patients who have the hypersensitivity condition فرط تحسس, meaning it should not be used in individuals with this allergic disease.
